panelists: Julia Hoggett, CEO London Stock Exchange
stories_covered: Will Revolut and Monzo list on London Stock Exchange
Stamp duty as a perverse tax on UK stock investment
UK capital market competitiveness vs US listings
Pension fund reform to drive domestic capital investment
Regulatory changes to listing rules and research incentives
UK regulatory disconnection from retail and institutional investors
harry_takes: The UK has structurally disconnected society from capital markets through 20-30 years of regulatory and pension reforms
Stamp duty is a counterproductive tax that penalizes UK investors and companies compared to US/European alternatives
The narrative that US listings are superior is empirically false: 9 of 20 large UK companies that listed in US have already delisted, only 4 trading up
UK capital markets offer better liquidity and index inclusion than commonly perceived, with free-float turnover in London exceeding S&P and NASDAQ
harry_boldest_quote: Stem duty is a perversity in the UK.
predictions: Julia: UK pension funds have committed to investing 5% of assets in private companies by 2030, predominantly in UK
Julia: If regulatory and pension reforms are executed, UK will become the default choice for great UK companies at significant scale
Julia: Revolut and Monzo will likely list in London rather than US if proper capital market incentives are in place
disagreements: Harry gently challenges Julia's claims on UK competitiveness (e.g., questioning whether Deliveroo or companies at Revolut/Monzo's scale would be valued differently in US), but these are exploratory rather than fundamental disagreements. Julia systematically reframes the narrative with data.
harry_role: Harry moderates as a skeptical but receptive interviewer, asking probing questions and occasionally challenging Julia's market-competitiveness claims. He sets the episode's anti-US-narrative frame but defers to Julia's expertise on market structure and data. He plays devil's advocate without taking a hard position.
notable: Episode recorded at LSE headquarters with the LSE CEO; the interview is entirely one-on-one (no Jason Lemkin or Rory O'Driscoll). Julia makes a data-driven case for UK market competitiveness using specific examples (ARM, Wise, TransferWise) to directly counter the media narrative.
